sentences.text: My name is Martha Frazer, M-A-R-T-H-A F-R-A-Z-E-R.
Today is October 15, 1996.
I'm conducting an interview with Rose Winterfeldt in Yonkers, New York, United States of America, in the English language.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Rose Winterfeldt, R-O-S-E W-I-N-T-E-R-F-E-L-D-T.
Lehmann, L--E-H-M-A-N-N.
My birth date is September 9, 1927.
I'm 69 years old.
Berlin, Germany.
B-E-R-L-I-N, G-E-R-M-A-N-Y.
Right.
But I still remember very well.
In 1930, my parents moved into a luxury apartment in Lankwitz, which is a part of the city of Berlin.
And it was a-- a huge apartment, with a blue-- beautiful solarium, which impressed me, with plants and beautiful garden furniture.
And my grandma lived with us.
And we had a maid.
And everything was beautiful.
And we had a huge family gathering in the apartment for the holidays, birthdays, and so on.
And I was just a happy little child.
I started kindergarten probably when I was three and a half or four.
It was a private kindergarten.
I remember that very well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And my parents were happy people.
And we had a good life.
And I remember in later years that my mother told me that during that time, from 1930 to '33, she often had the thought that life is too good.
It can't stay that way.
And she, like, had a premonition, you know?
It was too good.
And-- Can you tell us a little more about your family, of your father and what he did?
Yeah.
Well, my-- my father was the general manager of the trade union for all the textile retailers in all of Germany.
And he was also the CEO of the same trade union for the retailers in the city of Berlin.
It was-- he was heading the legal department.
He was not a lawyer, but he had a legal education.
And he traveled all over Germany to represent textile retailers in any lawsuits or whatever might come up.
And that was what he did.
And he was very well paid.
He had a very high salary.
He-- he started working there in I believe 1921 or 1920.
I'm not quite sure.
And by the time I was born in '27, he was already all the way up there in a very leading and prominent position.
He-- yes, yes.
He was in World War I.
He was-- he made it to the rank of master sergeant.
[LAUGHTER]
And he was in the-- in the Russian front.
And he always told the stories that he was put in charge of a small Jewish town in-- in Russia somewhere.
I don't know where.
And after the revolution in 1917, and even before, they had a great life there, you know?

The-- the-- the Jews of Russia at that time were looking forward for the Germans to come because they were under such pressure and pogroms there, that the Germans seemed to them at that time as liberators.
That's a story my father told me.
[LAUGHS] Now, your grandmother lived with you.
This was your father's mother?
My father's mother.
She lived with us since 1930, since we moved into that big apartment, yes.
Yeah.
My father had two brothers and-- wives.
And they had wives and children.
And of course, my mother's family was also in Berlin.
And there was an extended family of cousins.
And my grandma had brothers and sisters, so it was a huge family.
Running the household, yes.
That was my-- that's what my mother did, yes.
Very Jewish.
I would call it conservative.
It was a kosher household.
And the kitchen was separated into two parts, you know?
And my-- my mother did all the cooking, of course.
The maid did only the other cleaning work.
My mother did the cooking, the baking, everything.
And every Shabbos, we went to temple, and observed the holidays very strictly.
And we were just a real Jewish family.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yeah.
He--
He was.
As a very young man-- I think in the beginning of World War I, when all the men were drafted and he had not reached that age yet, the cantor in their little community was drafted.
And he started to learn.
He went, actually, to school.
And-- and he became the cantor there.
And then later on, he expanded and expanded.
And on all High Holidays, he gave his service to the Jewish orphanage in Berlin and performed the services there on the High Holidays.
No.
No, we did not.

It was-- mostly we selected the synagogue to go to where we wanted to hear a particular rabbi or listen to a particular cantor, because the rabbis and the cantors switched also from one synagogue to the other in Berlin.
And then also, my uncle was a-- a-- has a-- a synagogue choir.
And we went sometimes where he was with his choir.
So we were not bound to one particular synagogue.
That is not done, anyway, and it wasn't at that time in Germany.
Everyone went to different synagogues, except for the High Holidays.
You had to make up your mind before where you want to go because you needed tickets.
No.
I-- I wouldn't even know at that time.
I think mostly non-Jewish, because I don't even remember having met a Jewish family out there.
I don't-- I wouldn't know.
It was different later, when we moved to another place.
But at that time, no, I don't think so.
Yes, yes.
This apartment building was mostly non-Jewish, so-- didn't make any difference.
Nobody cared.
Everyone.
All the kids in kindergarten were my friend.
I was invited-- and attached to that kindergarten was a girl's high school.
And the-- the senior class had to take care of the little girls in the kindergarten sometimes.
And I was invited to their homes-- very elegant homes, you know?
Nobody cared whether I was Jewish or not Jewish.
Nobody asked.
The first thing I-- I remember was that my father was kicked out of one of his positions, and that he said we have to give up this luxury apartment.
We can't afford it.
And in 1933, we moved.
We moved to Marienfelde, into a small house, which was like little houses in-- in a row, like you find in Queens or Brooklyn.
They're private houses, but you rented them.
It was very small and very cheap.
And that's where we moved in '33-- in December of '33 already.
Right away, because he was-- he was representing the German textile industry.
Now, how can a Jew represent the German textile-- so he-- he was cut off right away-- right away in April, when Hitler came to power.
They kept him on for a while for other jobs, but we can talk about that.
[LAUGHS]
Of course.
Of-- of course.
Of course, yes.
Of course, yes, my grandma lived with us always.
I started school in April of 1934 in Marienfelde, yeah, which was also part of Berlin.
It's just a section.
It was public school, yes.
Well, I was the only Jewish child in that school.
There was only one other Jewish family in Marienfelde, but they had grown children.
So I was the only child.
And right from-- right from the beginning, nobody played with me or talked with me in the schoolyard, you know?
I was alone.
But I somehow didn't mind.
I was-- i-- it must have been the influence from home that I feel better than the others.
I don't know.
I-- I didn't-- I didn't suffer at that time.
I didn't realize that I suffered.
Of course, I did suffer.
But I took it that's a fact.
Nobody wants me.
They put ugly notes on my desk.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Didn't bother me much.
What about the teachers?
How did they treat you?
Well, some teachers were just OK.
They were correct.
They didn't harm me.
But we had one teacher in particular.
Every lesson we had with him, when the lesson started, I had to march up in front of the class and had to state, I am a Jewish child, and then march back to my seat and sit down.
And a while later, after the war, I found out what happened to that teacher, because I would have done something to him.
But anyway-- [LAUGHS]
Herr Schaefer.
One of the Russians shot him.
He must have been really some piece of work, you know?
Yeah.
In the neighborhood, nobody talked to us except our direct neighbors, who were Swedes.
It was a Swedish family living right in the next house.
They were very kind and friendly to us.
And they had a little baby.
And I was allowed to watch that little baby.
But the other people, nobody even talked to us.
And-- but one day, a boy of my class caught me on the street and started to beat me up.
And he threw me on the floor and he trampled on me, stepped on me.
And out came a man of one of these little houses-- come-- he came running over, pulled that boy's pants down, and gave them a whipping.
And that was the first time that somebody showed to me they're not all bad, you know?
And, well, that was that.
[LAUGHS] What was your understanding at this time as to why these things were happening?
How did a child your age grasp this?
What did your parents tell you?
Well-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what did they tell me?
Be proud to be a Jew and always wear your head up.
And by the way, going back to my first year in school-- school started April 1.
I know that for a fact.
And on April 20, they celebrated Hitler's birthday.
So we little kids, first graders, and everybody else were marched into the schoolyard.
And they-- they-- they pulled up their flag.
And they started singing their-- their-- their songs.
And I was in the end of the line.
And I walked out of the schoolyard and walked home.
And my mom said, what-- what are you doing here?
And I told her, I don't want to be there when they sing their songs.
And I was six and a half years old.
See, that's-- that's how it was.
I never walked down with my head or afraid.
Never, never.
Were you exposed to Hitler on the radio or other means of propaganda as to what was being said about the Jews?
Well, I-- I know there was a so-called Sturmerkasten put out that was like a-- a-- a publication board, where they had pictures, and how they pictured Jews and what Jews would do, you know?
I just walked by.
It doesn't-- it's not true.
Didn't bother me.
Well, by the end of '35, my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father had lost all-- his job completely.
He had no work.
And we were actually devastated.
We became very poor.
And my mother's family-- we have to talk about my mother-- helped us a great deal.
And my father did all kinds of odd jobs here and there, and just to make a few pennies, so to speak.
They were very, very hard years.
And my mother wanted to leave Germany, of course.
But my father said, that can't last.
I'm a law-abiding citizen.
I haven't done anything wrong.
I'm not going to leave.
That's what that was.
Right.
I-- I actually don't know how it happened.
But in-- in 1935, when the Nuremberg laws came out, all of a sudden, there were discussions in my family about these laws.
And-- and I realized that somehow, these laws affected us, affected my mother.
And then slowly, but surely I learned that my mother was not born Jewish.
I didn't know that until then.
It was never mentioned or-- it was no reason.
All the Jewishness in-- in-- in the house I learned from my mother.
And she kept the kosher house, and she lit the Shabbos candles, and-- and--
Of course.
Yes.
Everything.
To-- to think-- and-- of course not.
And my-- my grandparents, I knew they had a Christmas tree.
But I did not.
I was too young to make a connection.
I didn't think that my mother at one time must have had a Christmas tree too, you know?
It never occurred to me.
And yeah, I learned.
I learned that my mother was-- had-- had converted to Judaism, and that she was very proud of it.
And--
Well, she-- she had to go three times to the rabbi, or to a-- a-- a consortium of rabbis, and to ask for being converted to Judaism.
And this third time, of course, they accepted her.
And then she went for lessons.
And she learned Hebrew.
And my father, of course, helped her.
And she went to the synagogue.
And-- and her Hebrew was better than mine.
[LAUGHS]
And I went to a Jewish school, you know?
She was-- she was absolutely-- it's amazing.
She was an amazing woman.
Now, then she married your father about--
She married--
--five months after her conversion.
Right, after the conversion was certified.
Yes, yes.
They met-- my-- where my father worked at this trade union, my mother was a very young secretary there.
She was there before he came there.
And he saw her, and he fell in love with this sweet young girl.
And since he was in a-- in a higher position-- and-- and she also fell in love with him, of course, I have to say-- she left then, right away.
After they discovered their love for each other, she left right away and found another position, you know?

My mother's family embraced my father right from the beginning, actually, a few more months after they had met, while my father's family was very much against it, that he was even befriended with a non-Jewish girl, you know?
And my parents told me that they actually had never thought that they would be able to get married.
But they wanted to stay somehow together all their lives, marriage or no marriage.
But then slowly and surely, the family came around.
And when my mother converted to Judaism and went for months and months and months to the rabbis for teaching, my grandma took her under her-- what do you call this?
Supervision-- and taught her all the cooking, and the kosher, and everything.
So-- and my grandma and my mother were very close.
Can you just tell us what your mother's parents said after meeting your father?
Oh, yeah.
My-- my-- my mother brought my father to his parents to introduce him.
And my grandfather had, of course, a nice conversation with my father.
And then he said, with this man, you can walk to the end of the earth.
And that was that.
That family-- her parents had accepted him fully.
Not at that time.
Not right in the beginning.
Later, when I-- I went in later to the Jewish school-- and people-- or children somehow found out that my mother was not born Jewish, because being Aryans was a big, big thing, you know?
The Gestapo constantly sent forms to the teachers to fill out for each child.
And then you had to say what your-- and-- what your parents are.
And I had probably to say that my mother is Aryan, and I didn't.
I said, my mother-- my parents are Jewish.
And the teacher said, but your mother isn't Jewish.
She is Aryan.
And I started crying.
That was-- that was, to me, horrible to say that to me, that my mother wasn't Jewish.
I only knew her as being Jewish.
Right.
I was considered, according to the Nuremberg laws-- I was called a Geltungsjude.
That means I am considered a Jew because I was brought up in the Jewish religion, of course.
Oh, of--
Of course.
If I-- if my mother wouldn't have been Jewish, and I wouldn't have been brought up Jewish, then I would have been a-- a Mischling first grade, they called this.
What's a Mischling?
I don't even know how to translate it.
But I would not have to wear a star.
I did not want to have to do forced labor.
I would not have to quit school and all this, you know?
And that I was Jewish.
That was more-- that was more--
More Jewish than if she hadn't.
--that-- that-- that her being Jewish did not count.
Only her blood counted.
Her blood was Aryan.
But I was brought up Jewish.
And that, of course, brought the hardship onto me.
Absolutely, yes.
A category for everything.
My father didn't-- wouldn't even have to wear the star then if I would have not been brought up Jewish.
Yeah.
Right.
Well, as I said, my father did all kinds of odd jobs as a salesman.
And his brother had a little business-- a-- a paper-- paper-- how do you call this?
Oh, god.
Retail-- there is retail and there is--
Wholesale.
A little wholesale paper business.
My father helped there.
And we-- we helped at home.
He-- he-- he was selling napkins to restaurants.
And-- and they had to be folded.
And my-- and they-- my father brought home packs and packs of unfolded napkins.
And we would fold them at home to make a few pennies, you know, to help my uncle, but at the same time, to get a few pennies for our work-- my grandma, my mom, and myself, you know?
It was tough.
Right.
Well, my grandparents always helped in every respect.
And in-- in-- in the years when-- when it got really bad-- and my aunt, my mother's sister, and her husband-- she got married in 1939.
Her husband embraced us fully and helped in every way possible.
This man, my-- my uncle by marriage, would sit down to eat and would say, before I start eating, do Lehmanns-- meaning my family-- do they have something to eat today?
And when my aunt would tell him, yes, they do, then he would eat.
He was very special-- very special man, and came into the family in 1939.
Don't forget that.
My father had-- my-- my grandfather had-- he was a barber.
He had a barber shop, yes.
Oh, yes.
Sure, sure.
They helped us financially a-- a little bit.
I mean, it was not a big business.
They had-- they didn't have much.
But they always helped.
Whatever they had, they would share with us.
Always.
I was still in this--
Absolutely.
No, I couldn't-- I couldn't say that they-- they were bad enough as they were, you know?
Nothing-- the-- the situation at home worsened because we got-- we were poor.
We had hardly enough money to buy food, you know?
No, not yet.
Not yet, no.
No.
Mhm.
